questions.question: A function f: A → B is called injective (one-one) if:
options: A) f(x₁) = f(x₂) implies x₁ = x₂
B) Every element of B has a preimage in A
C) f(x₁) = f(x₂) for all x₁, x₂ ∈ A
D) The domain equals the codomain
questions.explanation: A function is injective (one-one) if distinct inputs give distinct outputs: f(x₁) = f(x₂) ⟹ x₁ = x₂ (equivalently, x₁ ≠ x₂ ⟹ f(x₁) ≠ f(x₂)). This ensures no two different elements map to the same image.

questions.question: The domain of f(x) = √(4 − x²) is:
questions.explanation: For f(x) = √(4 − x²), we need 4 − x² ≥ 0, so x² ≤ 4, giving −2 ≤ x ≤ 2. Domain = [−2, 2]. Both endpoints are included because at x = ±2, the expression equals zero (square root of zero is defined).

questions.question: If f(x) = 2x + 3, then f⁻¹(x) is:
questions.explanation: To find f⁻¹, let y = 2x + 3. Solving for x: 2x = y − 3, so x = (y − 3)/2. Therefore f⁻¹(x) = (x − 3)/2. Verify: f(f⁻¹(x)) = 2·(x−3)/2 + 3 = x − 3 + 3 = x ✓

questions.question: If f(x) = x² and g(x) = x + 1, then (f ∘ g)(x) is:
questions.explanation: (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = f(x + 1) = (x + 1)². Note the order: f ∘ g means apply g first, then f. Contrast with g ∘ f = g(f(x)) = g(x²) = x² + 1. Composition is generally NOT commutative.

questions.question: The range of f(x) = sin x is:
questions.explanation: sin x oscillates between −1 and 1, achieving both extreme values (at x = −π/2 and π/2 respectively). So the range is [−1, 1] (closed interval). Unlike domain which is (−∞, ∞) for sine, the range is bounded.

questions.question: Which function is its own inverse?
questions.explanation: f(x) = 1/x: f⁻¹(x) = 1/x = f(x). So it is its own inverse (an involution). f(x) = x² is not injective on ℝ (not invertible without restriction). f(x) = 2x has inverse x/2. f(x) = x+1 has inverse x−1.

questions.question: A surjective (onto) function f: A → B satisfies:
questions.explanation: A function is surjective (onto) if every element of the codomain B has at least one preimage in A, i.e., Range = B. It's possible for multiple elements in A to map to the same element in B. Surjective means nothing in B is 'left unmapped'.

questions.question: The domain of f(x) = log(x − 2) is:
questions.explanation: For logarithm, the argument must be strictly positive: x − 2 > 0, so x > 2. Domain = (2, ∞). Note: x = 2 gives log(0) which is undefined (log approaches −∞). The bracket would be wrong; use open interval.

questions.question: If f: ℝ → ℝ, f(x) = x³, this function is:
questions.explanation: f(x) = x³ is injective: if x₁³ = x₂³ then x₁ = x₂ (cube root is unique in ℝ). It is surjective: for every y ∈ ℝ, x = y^(1/3) ∈ ℝ exists with f(x) = y. Since both hold, f is bijective.

questions.question: If f(x) = eˣ, then f⁻¹(x) is:
questions.explanation: The inverse of the exponential function f(x) = eˣ is the natural logarithm f⁻¹(x) = ln x. This is because eˡⁿˣ = x and ln(eˣ) = x. The domain of ln x is (0, ∞) which is the range of eˣ.

questions.question: The composite function (g ∘ f)(x) means:
questions.explanation: (g ∘ f)(x) = g(f(x)): apply f first, then g to the result. The notation reads right to left. (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)): apply g first, then f. Always remember the inner function is applied first.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function from A = {1,2,3} to B = {a,b}?
questions.explanation: Option A maps element 1 to both a and b: (1,a) and (1,b). This violates the definition of a function — each element of the domain must map to exactly ONE element of the codomain. Options B, C, D are valid functions.

questions.question: The period of f(x) = sin(2x) is:
questions.explanation: The period of sin(nx) is 2π/n. For sin(2x), period = 2π/2 = π. The multiplier inside compresses the function horizontally by factor n. sin(x) has period 2π, cos(x) has period 2π, tan(x) has period π.

questions.question: If f(x) = (x − 1)/(x + 1), then f(f(x)) is:
questions.explanation: f(f(x)) = f((x−1)/(x+1)). Let t = (x−1)/(x+1). Then f(t) = (t−1)/(t+1) = ((x−1)/(x+1) − 1)/((x−1)/(x+1) + 1) = (x−1−x−1)/(x−1+x+1) = (−2)/(2x) = −1/x. Wait, let me recheck: numerator: (x−1)−(x+1) = −2; denominator: (x−1)+(x+1) = 2x. So f(f(x)) = −2/2x = −1/x. So answer is −1/x, i.e., option B is closest.

questions.question: The natural domain of f(x) = 1/√(x − 3) is:
questions.explanation: We need x − 3 > 0 (strictly, because it's under a square root AND in the denominator — cannot be zero). So x > 3. Domain = (3, ∞). If it were only √(x−3), domain would be [3,∞). But division by zero is undefined, so x = 3 is excluded.

questions.question: A bijective function f: A → B exists if and only if:
questions.explanation: A bijection (one-one and onto) between finite sets A and B exists if and only if they have the same number of elements. Injective ⟹ |A| ≤ |B|. Surjective ⟹ |A| ≥ |B|. Both together ⟹ |A| = |B|.

questions.question: If f(x) = 3x − 2, then f⁻¹(4) is:
The function f(x) = x² from ℝ to ℝ is:
questions.explanation: f(x) = x² is many-one (f(2) = f(−2) = 4, so not injective). The range is [0,∞) ≠ ℝ (codomain), so it's not surjective (into). Hence: many-one and into (not onto). If restricted to [0,∞) → [0,∞), it becomes bijective.

questions.question: The range of f(x) = eˣ is:
questions.explanation: eˣ is always positive (eˣ > 0 for all real x) and can get arbitrarily close to 0 (as x → −∞) but never equals 0. It can take any positive value. So range = (0, ∞). eˣ ≥ 1 only when x ≥ 0; the range over all ℝ is (0,∞).

questions.question: If f(x) = sin x and g(x) = x², then (f ∘ g)(π/2) is:
questions.explanation: (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = sin(x²). At x = π/2: (f ∘ g)(π/2) = sin((π/2)²) = sin(π²/4). Note π²/4 ≈ 2.47 radians, so sin(π²/4) ≈ sin(2.47) ≈ 0.618. This is NOT 1. A common mistake is computing sin²(π/2) = (g∘f)(π/2).

questions.question: Which of the following functions is even?
options: D) f(x) = x sin x + x
questions.explanation: A function is even if f(−x) = f(x). Check option A: f(−x) = (−x)² + cos(−x) = x² + cos x = f(x) ✓. Option B: f(−x) = −x + sin(−x) = −x − sin x = −f(x), so odd. x³ and x sin x + x are odd. So A is even.

questions.question: The inverse of f(x) = log₂ x is:
questions.explanation: If f(x) = log₂ x, let y = log₂ x. Then x = 2^y. Swapping x and y: y = 2^x. So f⁻¹(x) = 2ˣ. The inverse of a logarithm with base b is the exponential with base b. log₂ and 2ˣ are inverse functions.

questions.question: The function f(x) = |x| is:
questions.explanation: f(−x) = |−x| = |x| = f(x), so f is even. For odd function, we'd need f(−x) = −f(x), i.e., |x| = −|x|, which only holds at x = 0. So |x| is even but not odd. Graph is symmetric about y-axis.

questions.question: The number of functions from a set with m elements to a set with n elements is:
questions.explanation: Each of the m elements in the domain can independently map to any of the n elements in the codomain. By multiplication principle: n × n × ... × n (m times) = nᵐ. For bijections the count is different (requires m = n for existence).

questions.question: If f(x) = (x + 1)/(x − 1), the domain of f is:
questions.explanation: The function is undefined when the denominator = 0: x − 1 = 0, so x = 1. Domain = ℝ \ {1} = all reals except 1. The numerator (x + 1) = 0 at x = −1, but that just makes f(x) = 0, which is defined.

questions.question: Which of the following is a bijection from ℝ to ℝ?
questions.explanation: f(x) = x³ + x: f'(x) = 3x² + 1 > 0 always, so strictly increasing (hence injective). As x → ±∞, f(x) → ±∞, so surjective onto ℝ. Hence bijective. f(x) = x² is many-one. sin x and |x| are not injective on ℝ.

questions.question: The range of f(x) = 2 + 3sin x is:
questions.explanation: Since −1 ≤ sin x ≤ 1, multiply by 3: −3 ≤ 3sin x ≤ 3. Add 2: −1 ≤ 2 + 3sin x ≤ 5. Range = [−1, 5]. For f(x) = a + b·sin x: range is [a−|b|, a+|b|].

questions.question: The logarithmic function f(x) = logₐ x is defined for:
questions.explanation: logₐ x requires: x > 0 (argument must be positive), a > 0 (base must be positive), and a ≠ 1 (base ≠ 1 to avoid constant function). With a = 1, aˣ = 1 for all x, which has no meaningful inverse.

questions.question: If f(x) = √x and g(x) = x − 4, then the domain of (f ∘ g)(x) is:
questions.explanation: (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = f(x − 4) = √(x − 4). For this to be defined, x − 4 ≥ 0, so x ≥ 4. Domain = [4, ∞). The domain of a composite is restricted by the inner function's output matching the outer function's domain requirements.

questions.question: An odd function satisfies:
options: C) f(x) = f(x + T) for some T
questions.explanation: An odd function satisfies f(−x) = −f(x) — it is antisymmetric about the origin. Examples: sin x, x³, tan x. An even function satisfies f(−x) = f(x) — symmetric about the y-axis. A periodic function satisfies f(x) = f(x+T).

questions.question: If f: A → B and g: B → C are both bijective, then g ∘ f is:
questions.explanation: The composition of two bijections is always a bijection. If f and g are both injective, g∘f is injective. If both are surjective, g∘f is surjective. Both properties together give bijective. This is a fundamental theorem of function composition.

questions.question: The value of log₂ 8 + log₃ (1/9) is:
questions.explanation: log₂ 8 = log₂ 2³ = 3. log₃(1/9) = log₃(3⁻²) = −2. Sum = 3 + (−2) = 1. Key: logₐ(aⁿ) = n. Convert to powers of the base to evaluate logarithms quickly.

questions.question: The function f(x) = x²−4x+4 can be written as f(x) = (x−2)². Its minimum value is:
questions.explanation: f(x) = (x−2)² ≥ 0 always, and equals 0 when x = 2. So the minimum value is 0. Since the coefficient of x² is positive (+1), the parabola opens upward and has a minimum at its vertex (2, 0).

questions.question: If f(x) = x + 2 and g(x) = x², what is (g ∘ f)(3)?
questions.explanation: (g ∘ f)(3) = g(f(3)) = g(3 + 2) = g(5) = 5² = 25. First compute f(3) = 5, then apply g: g(5) = 25. This is different from (f ∘ g)(3) = f(9) = 11.

questions.question: The exponential function f(x) = aˣ (a > 1) is:
questions.explanation: For a > 1, aˣ is strictly increasing (grows as x increases). For 0 < a < 1, aˣ is strictly decreasing. For a = 1, it's constant (f(x) = 1). Exponential functions are never periodic (they go to 0 or ∞, never return).

questions.question: The domain of f(x) = sin⁻¹(2x) is:
questions.explanation: sin⁻¹(u) is defined for −1 ≤ u ≤ 1. For f(x) = sin⁻¹(2x), we need −1 ≤ 2x ≤ 1, giving −1/2 ≤ x ≤ 1/2. Domain = [−1/2, 1/2]. The argument of arcsin must always lie in [−1, 1].

questions.question: If f(x) = x² − 1 and g(x) = √x, then (g ∘ f)(x) is defined for:
questions.explanation: (g ∘ f)(x) = g(f(x)) = g(x²−1) = √(x²−1). For this to be defined, x²−1 ≥ 0, so x² ≥ 1, meaning x ≥ 1 or x ≤ −1. Domain = (−∞,−1] ∪ [1,∞).

questions.question: The function f(x) = logₐ x and g(x) = aˣ satisfy:
options: A) They are inverses of each other
C) They have the same domain
D) They have the same range
questions.explanation: f(x) = logₐ x and g(x) = aˣ are inverse functions. f(g(x)) = logₐ(aˣ) = x and g(f(x)) = a^(logₐ x) = x. Domain of logₐ x is (0,∞) while domain of aˣ is (−∞,∞) — they differ.

questions.question: For f(x) = (x² − 9)/(x − 3), what is the domain?
questions.explanation: Domain excludes values making denominator zero: x − 3 = 0 ⟹ x = 3. So domain = ℝ \ {3}. Note: (x²−9)/(x−3) = (x−3)(x+3)/(x−3) = x+3 for x ≠ 3. Even though it simplifies nicely, x = 3 is still not in the domain.

questions.question: A function f is called bijective if it is:
questions.explanation: A bijective function (one-one correspondence) is one that is both injective (one-one) AND surjective (onto). Bijections allow a perfect pairing between elements of domain and codomain. Only bijective functions have well-defined inverses.
